parts.prompt:
Identify one technological or military factor that contributed to the expansion of Muslim
empires such as the Ottoman, Safavid, or Mughal Empires during the period circa 1300 to
1600.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for addressing the prompt with historically defensible content.

required_elements: Gunpowder weapons (like cannons) contributed to the expansion of Muslim empires.
alternatives.correct_example: Gunpowder weapons (like cannons) contributed to the expansion of Muslim empires.

required_elements: Increasing centralization of the military, including standing armies, helped Muslim empires expand during the period circa 1300 to 1600.
alternatives.correct_example: Increasing centralization of the military, including standing armies, helped Muslim empires expand during the period circa 1300 to 1600.

required_elements: New military and bureaucratic recruitment methods, such as the Janissaries, helped Muslim empires like the Ottomans expand.
alternatives.correct_example: New military and bureaucratic recruitment methods, such as the Janissaries, helped Muslim empires like the Ottomans expand.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Restating the prompt without providing a defensible response.
Providing an example not relevant to the specific period or region asked.
common_traps: Identifies a person or event without explaining its historical relevance.
Provides multiple examples when only one is needed — focus on quality not quantity.

parts.prompt:
Explain one way that Muslim rulers during the period circa 1300 to 1600 used economic
policies to generate revenue for their states or empires.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for addressing the prompt with historically defensible content.

required_elements: Many Muslim rulers addressed the financial needs of their expanding empires by adopting various policies of tax-farming to increase revenues and ensure that tax revenues were collected.
alternatives.correct_example: Many Muslim rulers addressed the financial needs of their expanding empires by adopting various policies of tax-farming to increase revenues and ensure that tax revenues were collected.

required_elements: Some Muslim rulers used tribute collection as a means for both increasing revenue and controlling vassal states that had been defeated but not incorporated.
alternatives.correct_example: Some Muslim rulers used tribute collection as a means for both increasing revenue and controlling vassal states that had been defeated but not incorporated.

required_elements: Many Muslim rulers used innovative tax policies, including appointing tax officials (Mughal zamindars) or allowing local community leaders to collect the taxes for their entire community or locale. In other instances, Muslim rulers monetized taxes that had previously been collected in kind.
alternatives.correct_example: Many Muslim rulers used innovative tax policies, including appointing tax officials (Mughal zamindars) or allowing local community leaders to collect the taxes for their entire community or locale. In other instances, Muslim rulers monetized taxes that had previously been collected in kind.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Restating the prompt without providing a defensible response.
Providing an example not relevant to the specific period or region asked.
common_traps: Identifies a person or event without explaining its historical relevance.
Provides multiple examples when only one is needed — focus on quality not quantity.

parts.prompt:
Explain one reason why some Muslim rulers during the period circa 1300 to 1600 adopted
tolerant policies toward religious or ethnic minorities in their states or empires.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for addressing the prompt with historically defensible content.

required_elements: Some Muslim rulers adopted policies of tolerance towards religious minorities in their empires because it incited fewer revolts, which also helped establish the legitimacy of the rulers.
alternatives.correct_example: Some Muslim rulers adopted policies of tolerance towards religious minorities in their empires because it incited fewer revolts, which also helped establish the legitimacy of the rulers.

required_elements: Muslim rulers wanted to utilize the economic contributions of their minority populations, for example the jiziya taxes paid by non-Muslims, or the luxury goods traded by Greek, Armenian, or Jewish merchants.
alternatives.correct_example: Muslim rulers wanted to utilize the economic contributions of their minority populations, for example the jiziya taxes paid by non-Muslims, or the luxury goods traded by Greek, Armenian, or Jewish merchants.

required_elements: Muslim rulers wanted to utilize the military contributions of their minority populations, as seen, for example, in the Mughal use of Hindu Rajput warrior groups, or in the Ottoman use of Janissary troops recruited from its Christian minority groups.
alternatives.correct_example: Muslim rulers wanted to utilize the military contributions of their minority populations, as seen, for example, in the Mughal use of Hindu Rajput warrior groups, or in the Ottoman use of Janissary troops recruited from its Christian minority groups.

required_elements: Muslim rulers wanted to utilize the political contributions of their minority groups, as seen in the Ottoman sultans’ use of prominent members of the Greek community in the empire as provincial governors, or the Mughal emperors’ use of Hindu political advisors and ministers.
alternatives.correct_example: Muslim rulers wanted to utilize the political contributions of their minority groups, as seen in the Ottoman sultans’ use of prominent members of the Greek community in the empire as provincial governors, or the Mughal emperors’ use of Hindu political advisors and ministers.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Restating the prompt without providing a defensible response.
Providing an example not relevant to the specific period or region asked.
common_traps: Identifies a person or event without explaining its historical relevance.
Provides multiple examples when only one is needed — focus on quality not quantity.
